reasoning_summary: For live run 20260421_183606_bf16_gemm_v1_c03bcd3a at 25.755136 ms, the grouped_rows 4 -> 2 probe told us that CTA group depth was not the main remaining problem on the 3-stage surface. Compared with 4948b8ea, long_scoreboard stayed crushed at 0.22%, but runtime still got worse because barrier rose to 7.60%, mio_throttle rose to 4.57%, and the current 30464 B shared footprint still has to be drained through the same late steady-state seam. That makes the next best move a drain cleanup on the 3-stage path, not another launch-order cut. I keep an explicit rollback to the 2-stage PTX anchor second because it is now the clean fallback if the 3-stage late-drain fix still cannot beat the 25.06 ms anchor. I keep the writer-safe 256x128 reopen third so the structural 256x128 / 64x64 branch remains queued without interrupting the current local deconfounding work.
family_audit: accept: keep the 3-stage PTX family alive only for a late-drain cleanup, because the long_scoreboard win still looks real even though grouped_rows=2 lost.
accept: queue a clean two-stage PTX anchor restore as the fallback if the 3-stage drain cleanup still cannot beat the current 25.06 ms anchor.
defer_but_keep_open: leave the writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64 reopen in rank 3 so the structural branch stays available after the local PTX family is deconfounded.

directions.name: Split The Final 3-Stage PTX Drain Out Of The Late Steady-State Loop
directions.hypothesis: The 3-stage ring already solved the long_scoreboard problem, but the kernel still pays a full CTA sync on each late handoff while draining a larger in-flight shared-memory footprint. Pulling the no-refill drain into a narrower late path should keep the 3-stage latency win while trimming the extra barrier and mio_throttle tax that made both 4948b8ea and c03bcd3a lose.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Late-drain synchronization is now the clearest remaining local tax on the 3-stage PTX surface.
directions.risk: Moderate. This stays on the current PTX family and does not reopen tiling or dispatch, but it touches delicate wait_group drain logic and can still break correctness if mistimed.
ranking_notes: This is the smallest remaining change that preserves the only decisive benefit from the 3-stage experiment: long_scoreboard dropped from 5.45% to 0.22%.
The grouped_rows=2 probe says launch-order depth was not the fix, so the next narrow move is the late-drain seam itself.
If it loses, the result cleanly justifies rolling back to the two-stage PTX anchor without more ambiguity.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the late-drain split requires another stage-count rewrite, wider launch changes, or hot-loop predicates larger than a tiny late-path branch.
expected_local_changes: Split the late no-refill drain from the current 3-stage steady-state path.
Keep stage count at 3 and leave grouped_rows unchanged in the first pass.
Do not touch launch bounds or the accumulator schedule while testing the drain seam.

directions.name: Restore The Two-Stage PTX Anchor After The 3-Stage Probes
directions.hypothesis: If the late-drain cleanup still cannot hold a win, the clean fallback is to restore the current PTX two-stage anchor with grouped_rows=4 rather than keep stacking local fixes on a losing 3-stage surface. That anchor already measured 25.063408 ms with lower barrier and lower shared-memory footprint than the current branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: This is a fallback restore family, not a new bottleneck theory: it resets the search to the cleanest recent PTX anchor so later branches can be tested from a lower-variance surface.
directions.risk: Low. This is a bounded rollback to a recent measured surface, and it does not widen scope beyond the active PTX kernel.
ranking_notes: This is the clean fallback if the 3-stage drain cleanup still loses, because the two-stage anchor is already remeasured and understood.
It prevents the loop from getting trapped in a losing 3-stage branch just because the long_scoreboard win looked attractive.
It is ranked second because the 3-stage family still deserves one narrow sync-local rescue before a full rollback.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if restoring the two-stage anchor requires additional family changes beyond removing the extra stage and returning grouped_rows to 4.
expected_local_changes: Restore the PTX a_shared and b_shared ring from 3 stages back to 2.
Keep the active PTX dispatch and launch bounds unchanged while returning to the measured two-stage anchor.

directions.name: Reopen 256x128 64x64-Warp Hot-Band Tiling On The Dominant Surface
directions.hypothesis: The current PTX family is still well above the official best custom result, and even the cleaned-up two-stage anchor stays capped at 16.60% active warps. The source already contains a 256x128 hot-band path, and the persistent human guidance still points to 256x128 block tiling with 64x64 warp tiling and Right-Left-Right-Left reuse. That structural branch should remain queued while the local PTX family finishes deconfounding.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The 128x128 PTX surface may still be hitting a real tiling and warp-reuse ceiling even after local pipeline cleanup.
directions.risk: High. This is a broader geometry reopen with known correctness and performance history, and it touches launch routing, writer ownership, and export layout together.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
ranking_notes: This keeps the 256x128 / 64x64 guidance alive without interrupting the current local PTX diagnosis too early.
It is still the highest-ceiling structural branch left if the PTX family keeps oscillating around the same 25 ms plateau.
Rank 3 is deliberate because the current branch still has one narrow 3-stage fix and one clean two-stage fallback ahead of a broader reopen.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the reopen requires replaying a previously broken ownership or export path instead of a writer-safe 256x128 surface.
expected_local_changes: Reopen the fixed hot-band launch routing for the 256x128 dominant surface.
Keep writer ownership explicit and export-safe.
Preserve the 64x64 warp-tile reuse pattern instead of reviving the broken half-panel writer split.

notes: Round 6 treats the grouped_rows=2 probe as a negative result on top of a still-informative 3-stage family. The next move should either fix the late drain or restore the clean two-stage PTX anchor before another broad branch jump.
